---
title: "Declarative beats imperative"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
---

{{< include _setup.qmd >}}
{{< include _pages-nav.qmd >}}

## Declarative beats imperative

Tell the LLM what success looks like, not which buttons to press.

- **Imperative:** "Read file X, parse the JSON, filter to dates after Y, write to Z."
- **Declarative:** "Produce a CSV at Z containing only rows from X where date > Y. Tests should cover empty input, malformed JSON, and the date boundary."

Declarative prompts let the LLM bring its training data to bear. Imperative prompts pin it to your particular sequence and waste that.

This is also why **writing tests first** is so productive in this workflow: tests are declarative by nature. They state success, not procedure. If you can write the test, the LLM can write the implementation.
